actions.thought: It is Hand #1 of 150, playing Heads-Up. I am on the Button (SB) with 6h 7d, which is a weak offsuit connector (67o). At 100BB deep, GTO strategies typically fold this hand from the SB roughly 75-80% of the time, as it suffers from reverse implied odds and is often dominated. While playing in position is advantageous, starting the match by bloating the pot with the bottom of my range is unnecessary. A fold is the disciplined, standard play here to preserve chips for better spots.
K5o is a reasonable hand to open from the Button in a heads-up situation. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the Big Blind and utilize my positional advantage post-flop.

In a Heads-Up match, A4s is a strong hand defending the Big Blind. The opponent raised to 3x (300 chips). I need to call 200 more to win a total pot of 600, requiring 33% equity. A4s has significantly more equity than that against a standard button opening range. While a 3-bet is an option to apply pressure, calling allows me to realize equity and keep the opponent's range wide while playing out of position. The price is correct to defend.

On a board of 7h 8s 9d, the texture is very wet and connected, favoring straight draws and made hands. As the pre-flop caller in the Big Blind, standard strategy is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. My hand (As 4s) has Ace-high showdown value and a backdoor flush draw, but no immediate strong draw or pair. Leading out (donking) would likely only get action from better hands or fold out worse ones that might bluff later. Checking preserves my range and allows me to evaluate the opponent's action.

The board texture 7h-8s-9d is highly connected and favors the Big Blind's range, as they are more likely to have hands like 56, 6x, or TJ. With King-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6), I have some equity but don't want to build a pot on a board that hits my opponent's range so well. Checking back is the most prudent play to see a free turn and potentially realize my equity or showdown value.

The opponent checked back the flop on a very wet board (7h 8s 9d), which typically caps their range and suggests they do not have a strong overpair, set, or straight. The turn is the Ace of Diamonds (Ad), giving me top pair with a weak kicker (A4). This card heavily favors my range as the Big Blind defender. Leading out (probe betting) has merit to get value from draws or second pairs (9x, 8x), but betting risks folding out the bluffs that the opponent might attempt now that an Ace has hit. Furthermore, with a weak kicker, I am vulnerable if the opponent was pot-controlling a better Ace. Checking allows me to keep the opponent's range wide, potentially induce a bluff, and pot-control against better Aces. I plan to call a reasonable bet.

The Ace of Diamonds on the turn is a significant card that typically favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Although the flop (7-8-9) was very connected and better for the Big Blind's range, the Ace provides a good opportunity to represent a strong hand. However, having checked back the flop, checking the turn as well keeps the pot small and allows me to see a free river card. King-high has very little showdown value here, but checking back avoids being check-raised off the hand and preserves my stack in the early stages of the tournament.

I hold Top Pair (Aces) with a weak kicker on a board of 7h 8s 9d Ad 3h. The opponent's decision to check back both the flop (on a dynamic texture) and the turn (when the Ace hit) strongly caps their range, suggesting they lack a strong Ace, two pair, or set. While a value bet might target a sticky pair like 9x or 8x, the scary board texture (potential straights) makes it difficult for worse hands to call. Checking is the superior play to induce bluffs from missed draws or total air, and I intend to call a reasonable bet. If the opponent checks back, I likely win against their capped range.

The board has several straight possibilities (any 6 or 10) and an Ace. I have King-high with no pair. My opponent, the Big Blind, has checked through every street (flop, turn, and river). While my King-high has very little showdown value, checking back is the safest play. I missed my gutshot on the flop, and since I checked the turn when the Ace appeared, a river bluff is less credible. I'll check and take my chance at showdown, as the pot is small and I'd rather not risk more chips on a weak hand in the early stages of the tournament.

At 100bb deep in a Heads-Up match, T4s is a marginal hand on the Button. While suited hands generally have value, T4s is near the bottom of the playable range due to the poor kicker and lack of connectivity. Although I have position, the opponent showed aggression pre-flop in the previous hand (raising the BB), which suggests they might attack a limp. Folding here is a standard, low-variance play to preserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand even in a heads-up context. While I have the positional advantage post-flop, T2o does not realize its equity well and is often dominated. Given it is early in the session and I have plenty of chips, I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a more favorable opportunity.

I am on the Button with K2s. In heads-up play, K2s is a playable hand due to the high card value and flush potential. I have folded my previous button opportunities, so raising here establishes that I am willing to contest pots and prevents the opponent from realizing equity with random weak hands for free. A minimum raise to 200 is a standard size that balances risk and fold equity while allowing me to play in position if called.

Facing a 2x open from the Button/SB, T7o is a standard defending hand in the Big Blind. I am getting 3:1 pot odds, requiring only 25% equity to call profitably. Although I will be out of position post-flop, folding is too exploitable against a wide opening range.

On this flop of Ts-Jd-8c, I have flopped middle pair with a 7 kicker. This is a board that connects well with both players' ranges, particularly the pre-flop aggressor who will have more overpairs and strong Jacks. However, middle pair is a solid enough hand to check-call at least one street. I will start by checking to the aggressor to see how they evaluate the board texture. Leading (donking) here would likely reveal too much strength or vulnerability and doesn't serve much purpose against a standard wide button opening range.

I am in position on a wet board (Ts Jd 8c) that connects very well with the Big Blind's defensive range (straights, pairs, and draws). My hand (Kd 2d) has only King-high and a backdoor flush draw. Betting here is unlikely to generate enough folds from better hands, and I risk getting check-raised. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially hitting a King, a Diamond, or a straight draw on the turn, while keeping the pot small with a marginal holding.

The turn Queen is a significant card that likely connects with the pre-flop raiser's range. After the flop went check-check, my pair of tens remains a decent showdown hand but has dropped in relative strength. Betting now doesn't achieve much; better hands won't fold, and worse hands are unlikely to call. Checking allows me to control the pot and potentially see a cheap river or induce a bluff from missed overcards.

On the turn, the board is [Ts Jd 8c Qs]. I hold [Kd 2d]. The board texture is extremely connected; the sequence 8-T-J-Q means any 9 makes a straight, and I have an open-ended straight draw for the nuts (a 9 gives me the King-high straight 8-9-T-J-Q, and an Ace gives me the Broadway straight T-J-Q-K-A). Currently, I only have King-high, which has very little showdown value against any pair. Since the opponent checked, taking the free card is the most EV+ play. It allows me to realize my strong equity (8 outs to the nuts) without risking a check-raise or bloating the pot with a hand that is currently behind. If I hit my straight on the river, I can bet for value; if I miss, I save chips.

The river Ace is a very polarizing card on this board (Ts-Jd-8c-Qs-Ah). It completes straights for any King or any 9. My pair of Tens, which was middle pair on the flop, has significantly depreciated in value as the board developed. Since the flop and turn both went check-check, it's unlikely that either player has a monster, but my Tens are now beat by any Ace, Queen, Jack, or straight. There is no value in betting here, as better hands will not fold and worse hands will not call. I will check and hope to reach a cheap showdown.

I hold the Kd on a board of Ts Jd 8c Qs Ah. The combination of T-J-Q-K-A gives me the Broadway straight, which is the absolute nuts on this board (no flush or board pair possible). The opponent has checked to me on all three post-flop streets, suggesting a weak range or a trap, but the river card interacts heavily with ranges. If the opponent holds a 9, they have a lower straight and will likely call a large bet. If they hold an Ace, they have top pair and might call a value bet. Checking is not an option with the nuts. I will bet 300 (75% pot) to extract maximum value from lower straights and top pairs that might call, hoping to get paid off by a 9 or a curious Ax.

The board has become extremely dangerous with the Ace on the river, completing a possible straight for any King (T-J-Q-K-A) or any Nine (8-9-T-J-Q). My pair of Tens, which was middle pair on the flop, is now fourth pair on a board where almost any card in my opponent's range has likely overtaken me. Pro's bet of 300 into a 400 pot represents significant strength or a well-timed bluff on a scare card. Given that the flop and turn went check-check, Pro could easily have an Ace, a Jack, a Queen, or a 9 that was checking for pot control or a realized draw. My hand is a very weak bluff-catcher here, and there are better spots to commit chips. I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

In a heads-up tournament setting with deep stacks, completing the small blind with a hand like 6c 4d is a standard play to see a cheap flop. Although the hand is weak, the cost of 50 chips to potentially see a board where I can outplay the opponent or hit a straight/two-pair is worth the small investment.

I am in the Big Blind with [Kc Jd]. The opponent (Button/Small Blind) has limped for 100. KJo is a strong hand heads-up, with good high-card value. Facing a limp, I have the option to isolate with a raise or check to see a free flop. While raising to take down the pot pre-flop or build value is a viable aggressive line, KJo is unsuited and can be difficult to play out of position in a bloated pot if called. Checking allows me to keep the opponent's range wide (including many hands I dominate like Kx or Jx with worse kickers) and realize my equity without investing more chips pre-flop. Given the opponent has shown willingness to raise in previous hands, this limp likely indicates a marginal hand, making a check a solid, low-variance play.

I hold KcJd on a board of 5h7c8d. I am out of position in the Big Blind after a pre-flop limp. This board texture (connected low cards) hits a typical limping range (connectors, small pairs) harder than my broadway hand. I have two overcards and reasonable showdown value with King-high, but betting here likely only gets action from better hands (pairs, straight draws) and folds out worse hands. Checking is the best option to control the pot size, attempt to realize equity, and potentially call a small bet or see a free turn card.

I have flopped a straight (4-5-6-7-8) on a rainbow board. This is a very strong hand, only currently beaten by 9-6. Since the board is coordinated and there are many cards that could come on the turn to kill my action or potentially give my opponent a better hand (like a 9), I need to bet for value immediately. I will bet 150 into the 200 pot to get called by 8x, 7x, 5x, or straight draws.

I hold King-Jack offsuit on a low, connected board of 5-7-8. The opponent limped pre-flop and then bet 150 into a pot of 200 (75% pot). This board texture strongly favors a limping range (connectors, small pairs). While I have two overcards, I am out of position and facing a substantial bet that denies me the correct odds to chase a King or Jack (approx. 12% chance to hit on the turn vs requiring 30% equity to call). Calling would leave me guessing on future streets without a made hand. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with 7h 5c, which is a weak offsuit hand (7-5 offsuit). In a heads-up match, while ranges are wider, 75o is generally below the threshold for a profitable open-raise or limp without specific reads on the opponent's tendencies to over-fold. Previously, I folded 67o and T4s on the button, establishing a somewhat standard opening range. Raising here would be a pure bluff with poor post-flop playability if called. Limping is marginal. The most prudent play with this specific holding is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Qs6d in a heads-up match. Queen-six offsuit is a standard opening hand in this position. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage, testing the Big Blind's willingness to defend.

I hold Q4o in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the opponent on the Button. The pot odds require roughly 33% equity to call (200 into 400), and while Q4o has raw equity around 43% against a wide opening range, it suffers from poor playability and reverse implied odds out of position. The 3bb sizing makes defense tighter compared to a min-raise. Without specific reads that the opponent plays very poorly post-flop, defending this hand is -EV. I will fold.

I hold 2h 4c in the Small Blind/Button. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em (unsuited, low, unconnected). Although playing from the button in heads-up is generally advantageous, this hand falls into the bottom range that should be open-folded. I have folded junk hands from the SB previously (Hands 1, 3, 7), and while I need to be careful not to over-fold, 42o is simply not profitable to play against a competent opponent who might raise over a limp or defend against a min-raise.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with 5c 3d, which is a very weak starting hand. While I have positional advantage post-flop, the hand lacks connectivity and high-card value. Given the tournament context and the fact that I want to minimize losses with trash hands, I will fold this and wait for a more playable hand to exploit my opponent's tendencies.

I hold Qd3h (Queen-Three offsuit) on the Button (Small Blind). In Heads-Up play, while ranges are wide, Q3o is statistically one of the weaker hands (bottom 35-40%). Reviewing previous hands, I have folded similar weak hands like 67o, T4o, 75o, and 24o on the button (Hands 1, 3, 7, 9). Although the opponent has folded their Big Blind a couple of times (Hand 4, 10), my current strategy involves maintaining a reasonable opening frequency and not opening 'trash' hands to avoid difficult post-flop spots with poor equity. Folding is the standard play here to conserve chips.

I am holding 3c 2s, which is the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Although I am on the button and the cost to call is only 50 chips, this hand has almost no post-flop potential and very little equity if called. Given the tournament structure and the fact that I have plenty of chips, there is no need to play the literal worst hand in the deck. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

In heads-up play, Ace-Eight offsuit is a strong hand (top 20% range) and well above the average winning hand. Being on the Button (Small Blind), I have position post-flop. I want to build the pot and take the initiative rather than limping. A standard min-raise to 200 is appropriate to pressure the Big Blind while keeping the pot manageable relative to the stack depth of ~100 big blinds.

I am in the Big Blind with K3o. Pro has raised to 200 from the button, which is a min-raise. I only need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 4:1 pot odds. In heads-up play, K3o is strong enough to defend against a min-raise. I will call to see a flop.

I have bottom pair with a King kicker on a board of Qc-9c-3c. Since I am in the Big Blind and was the caller pre-flop, standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. My hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable to any higher pair or draws. I will check to see how the opponent acts.

I hold Ace-high (Ac 8d) on a Qs 9c 3c board. As the pre-flop raiser in position, I have a range advantage, but this specific board texture connects well with a Big Blind defending range (Qx, 9x, flush draws). I hold the Ace of clubs, which blocks the nut flush draw, but my hand has showdown value as Ace-high. Betting here might isolate me against better hands or force me to fold to a check-raise. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, control the pot size, and potentially use my Ace-high to bluff-catch or win at showdown against missed draws or weaker high cards. I will check to see the turn for free.

On the turn 7d, the board remains relatively safe. I have bottom pair with a King kicker. The flop went check-check, which suggests that Pro might not have a strong Queen or is playing a marginal hand cautiously. I will check again to see if Pro bets; my goal is to reach showdown cheaply or catch a bluff, as my hand has some showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value here.

On the turn, the board is 3c 9c Qs 7d. The 7d connects somewhat with the board (straight draws like J-10, 8-6), but my Ace-high still holds showdown value. I hold the Ac, blocking the nut flush draw, which reduces the likelihood my opponent is on that specific draw, but they could have other draws or a weak pair. Flash has checked twice, signaling weakness or a draw. Betting here turns my hand into a bluff, likely folding out worse hands while getting called by pairs that beat me. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, keeps the pot small, and lets me potentially win at showdown or re-evaluate on the river. I will take the free card.

I have bottom pair with a King kicker. The pre-flop aggressor has checked both the flop and the turn, which typically indicates a lack of strength or a willingness to go to showdown cheaply with a marginal hand. The river 10h doesn't change my relative hand strength much, though it does complete some unlikely straight draws (J8, 86). Since I have some showdown value but am unlikely to be called by worse if I bet, I will check and hope to win at showdown or see if Pro attempts a late bluff.

I hold Ace-high (Ac 8d) on a board of 3c 9c Qs 7d Th. The river Ten completes possible straights (KJ, J8) and gives anyone holding a Ten a pair. My opponent checked the flop, turn, and river, signaling weakness or a missed draw, but the river card connects heavily with a Big Blind defending range. My Ace-high has very limited showdown value as I lose to any pair, and most high-card hands (like Kx or Jx) have likely improved to a pair or straight. However, bluffing is risky because my line (check flop, check turn) doesn't credibly represent a strong hand that can pressure a pair of 3s, 7s, or Ts to fold in a small pot. Checking back guarantees I realize my remaining equity for free without risking more chips in a spot where I am likely behind but possibly beating a busted lower flush draw.

I am on the button with 8c6s. In a heads-up situation, 8-6 offsuit is a marginal hand but generally worth playing from the small blind to maintain pressure and see a flop. The pot is currently 150 with 50 more for me to call. Given my stack size and the stage of the tournament, I will complete the small blind to see a flop and evaluate from there, rather than raising and bloating the pot with a weak hand or folding and giving up equity.

In the Big Blind with K6s facing a limp from the Small Blind. I have no money to put in to see the flop. K6s is a decent hand heads-up, but raising out of position against a limp can be risky as it builds a pot where I might be dominated by better Kings or forced to fold to a 3-bet/limp-raise. Checking allows me to realize my equity for free and play a pot with a suited King. The opponent has shown a mix of limping and raising, so I will take the free flop.

I am out of position in a limped pot with King-high and a backdoor flush draw on a dry Q-3-2 board. In heads-up play, the Big Blind acts first post-flop. My hand has some showdown value, but not enough to value bet, and turning it into a bluff here by leading out likely only folds out hands I am already beating (like lower high cards). Checking allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially call a small bet or re-evaluate on the turn.

The flop is 2d-3s-Qh. I have 8c6s, which is total air. My opponent, Pro, checked from the big blind. In this heads-up scenario, this board is quite dry. A check from Pro often indicates a weak hand or a desire to control the pot. Since I have no showdown value, I can either check back to see a free card or try to take the pot down now with a small continuation bet. However, given the lack of connectivity and the low stakes of the pot, checking back and keeping the pot small is a safer way to preserve chips until I find a better spot to bluff or value bet. I will check.

I am in the Big Blind with K6s in a limped pot. The flop was checked through. The turn brings a 4c, making the board 2d-3s-Qh-4c. This puts three to a straight on the board (2-3-4). I now hold a gutshot straight draw (a 5 gives me a 2-3-4-5-6 straight) and King-high, which currently has some showdown value in a battle of blinds where ranges are wide. Leading out here is unlikely to get value from worse hands and might fold out hands I beat or induce a raise. Checking allows me to see a river cheaply or for free, potentially hitting my straight or winning at showdown with King-high if the opponent also checks.

The turn 4c adds a straight draw to the board texture, but it doesn't improve my 8-high. Pro has checked twice, which typically indicates weakness, but my hand has almost no showdown value. While I could attempt a bluff here to take down the small pot, checking back is a lower-risk play that allows me to see the river for free. Given the passive nature of the hand so far, I will check and see if I can improve on the river or reach showdown cheaply.

I am out of position in a limped pot with King-high (Kh6h). The board is 2d-3s-Qh-4c-Tc. The action has been checked through on the flop and turn. My King-high has some showdown value in a blind-vs-blind battle where ranges are wide and the opponent has shown passivity by checking twice. Betting would likely be a bluff to fold out Ace-high or small pairs, but given the small pot and the likelihood of being called by any pair, the risk-reward ratio is not favorable. Betting for value is impossible as worse hands (worse high cards) will fold. Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply; if the opponent checks back, I may win against lower high cards. If the opponent bets, I can easily fold.

The board has completed with a 10 of clubs, making the final texture 2d-3s-Qh-4c-Tc. I have 8-high, which has almost zero showdown value. Pro has checked for the third consecutive time on the river after a limped pre-flop. While my hand is weak, the pot is only 200 chips. Attempting to bluff here would require a bet to represent at least a pair or better, but given the passive nature of the hand and the small pot size, it is more prudent to check and concede the pot rather than risk chips on a low-stakes bluff. I will check and see the showdown.

In Heads-Up play with 100BB stacks, Q6o is a playable hand from the Button (SB), falling within the top 50-60% of hands. Folding here would be too tight given I have folded several previous buttons (Hands 1, 3, 7, 9, 11). While raising to 200 is a standard option to deny equity and seize initiative, my opponent 'Flash' has shown a tendency to defend the Big Blind against raises (Hands 5, 13) and check down post-flop. Limping (Calling) allows me to see a flop cheaply, keeps the pot manageable for a marginal hand, and utilizes my positional advantage to realize equity or bluff post-flop against a passive opponent. I will complete the small blind.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Five offsuit. The Small Blind has completed the bet, and I have the option to check or raise. K5o is a decent hand in heads-up play, but since I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand, I prefer to check and see a free flop. Raising here might bloat the pot with a marginal hand against a range that could contain traps or hands that dominate mine. Checking keeps the pot small and allows me to realize my equity.

The flop is paired with Jacks (Js Jc) and an Eight (8d). I have King-high. In a limped pot, there is little incentive to bloat the pot with a hand that has some showdown value but is not yet a made hand. Pro checked, and checking back allows me to see a free turn card while keeping the pot small, which is consistent with a pot-control strategy in this position.

I hold Queen-high on a paired board [8d Js Jc]. The pot is small (200) after a limped pre-flop sequence. My opponent 'Flash' has demonstrated extreme passivity in previous hands (Hands 2, 13, 14), frequently checking down entire boards. Q-high has legitimate showdown value here against a random Big Blind range. Betting likely only folds out hands I am already beating (lower high cards) and isolates me against pairs or trips. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, keep the pot manageable, and potentially win at showdown or improve on the turn without risking chips unnecessarily.

I am holding King-high in a limped pot where the flop was checked through. The turn 3h is a blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. King-high retains some showdown value in this scenario, especially given the passive play so far. There is little benefit to betting here, as I'm unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call. Checking allows me to continue controlling the size of the pot and potentially reach showdown or see a free river card.

I hold Queen-high on the Turn with the board reading [8d Js Jc 3h]. The 3h is a brick that doesn't change the dynamic significantly. Given the limped pot and my opponent 'Flash's' passive tendencies (frequently checking down), my Q-high has decent showdown value. Betting would likely only fold out hands I am already beating (worse high cards) while keeping in hands that beat me (Pairs, Ace-high, King-high). Checking back allows me to exercise pot control, realize my equity for free, and potentially win at showdown or hit a pair on the river without bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

In a limped pot where both players have checked every street, my King-high (Kc 5h) retains some showdown value. The board (8d Js Jc 3h 9d) is relatively disconnected, though any pair or Ace-high beats me. Betting is unlikely to get many better hands to fold and will almost never be called by worse hands. Checking allows me to realize my showdown value for free and is the most prudent play in this small, passive pot.

The hand has been checked down to the river in a limped pot. I hold Queen-high [Qc 6h] on a board of [8d Js Jc 3h 9d]. My opponent 'Flash' has shown a consistent tendency to check down hands and get to showdown passively (Hands 2, 13, 14), suggesting they may be a 'calling station' type player who won't easily fold a pair or even Ace-high/King-high to a river bet. While Q-high is weak, it beats missed draws (like T7 or 76). Betting would likely only fold out hands I am already beating and get called by better (Pairs, A-high, K-high). Since the pot is small (200 chips) and my opponent is passive, the best play is to check back, realize my equity for free, and hope Q-high is good, rather than risking chips on a low-percentage bluff.
I am on the button with 7h6c. This is a standard hand to open in heads-up play to put pressure on the big blind. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s 2d, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em. My opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 (3x the big blind). While the pot odds are 33% (calling 200 to win a pot of 600), my hand equity against a typical raising range is significantly lower (around 25-28%). Furthermore, I would be playing out of position post-flop with a hand that has very poor playability and reverse implied odds. Against a 3x raise, defending with 4-2 offsuit is a losing play. The correct strategic decision is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with J7o, a marginal but playable hand in heads-up poker (roughly 52% equity against a random hand). Given the deep stacks (100BB) and the opponent's tendency to defend their Big Blind against raises (Hand 5, 13), raising might simply bloat the pot with a mediocre hand. Limping (completing to 100) is a low-variance line that allows me to utilize my position post-flop against a wide range, consistent with my play in Hand 15 with Q6o. Folding is too conservative given the pot odds (3:1) and position.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Ten offsuit. The Small Blind (Pro) has just completed the bet (limped). KTo is a strong starting hand in heads-up play and is likely ahead of a standard limping range. I will raise to 400 total to build the pot and take the initiative, rather than checking and seeing a free flop.

I limped from the Small Blind (Button) with J7o, and the Big Blind raised to 400 (4BB). This is a large isolation raise (4x). To call, I need to put in 300 chips into a pot that will be 800, offering 37.5% pot odds. Against a reasonable isolation range (top 20-30% of hands), J7o has approximately 34-38% equity. While I have the advantage of position post-flop, J7o suffers from reverse implied odds (often dominated by better Jacks). Since this is the first time the opponent has isolated over a limp and the sizing is large, the disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot or a cheaper price.

In heads-up play, I want to maintain a high level of aggression from the button/small blind. While 5d 3h is a weak hand, I have seen 'Pro' fold to pre-flop raises several times in the last 17 hands. I will place a standard 3x raise to 300 to try and take down the pot immediately.

My opponent raises to 300 (3bb) from the button. I hold 5h 6c (56 offsuit) in the Big Blind. I need to call 200 into a total pot of 600, giving me 33% pot odds. While 56o has some straight potential, it is a very weak hand to play out of position, especially against a larger 3x raise. Without a significant post-flop skill advantage or history suggesting the opponent folds too much to aggression later, calling here is likely -EV. I will fold to preserve chips.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with Ace-Seven offsuit (A7o). In heads-up play, an Ace is a strong holding, often dominating the opponent's range. With 96.5 big blinds deep, I want to play this hand aggressively to take down the blinds immediately or play a pot in position. Folding is too weak for this hand strength. Limping invites the Big Blind to see a free flop or raise me. A min-raise to 200 is a standard size that balances my range, extracts value from worse hands that call, and folds out better equity hands than random junk. Given my previous history of raising similar hands (like A8o in Hand 13), this play is consistent.
